@graph.name: 9 Materials That Help When Child Loves Tight Wrapping
@graph.description: If your child wraps themselves so tight in blankets, squeezes into tiny spaces, and can't relax without pressure — their nervous system needs deep input to feel safe. It's not weird. It's regulation. Here are 9 materials that give them the squeeze their body is searching for.

mainEntity.name: Why does my child wraps themselves in blankets so tight, squeezes into tiny spaces, and always wants to be hugged as hard as possible. it's like their body needs constant pressure to feel okay?

acceptedAnswer.text: DEEP PRESSURE SEEKING / PROPRIOCEPTIVE UNDER-RESPONSIVITY! A sensory processing pattern where the child's nervous system requires deep, sustained pressure input to feel calm, organized, and secure. The proprioceptive system — which processes information from muscles, joints, and connective tissue — has a high threshold and needs intense input to register. Deep pressure activates the parasympathetic nervous system, creating a calming effect. Children with this pattern actively seek tight wrapping, compression, squeezing into spaces, and firm hugging because this input helps their brain feel where their body is and activates their calming system. Per SPD Foundation: Deep pressure touch activates the proprioceptive system and has a calming, organizing effect on the nervous system. Children who seek deep pressure are often under-responsive to proprioceptive input — their threshold is high, so they need more intense input to achieve the same regulatory effect that lighter touch provides for neurotypical children.

mainEntity.name: What is Deep Pressure Seeking / Proprioceptive Under-Responsivity?

acceptedAnswer.text: A sensory processing pattern where the child's nervous system requires deep, sustained pressure input to feel calm, organized, and secure. The proprioceptive system — which processes information from muscles, joints, and connective tissue — has a high threshold and needs intense input to register. Deep pressure activates the parasympathetic nervous system, creating a calming effect. Children with this pattern actively seek tight wrapping, compression, squeezing into spaces, and firm hugging because this input helps their brain feel where their body is and activates their calming system. Common signs include: Wraps self tightly in blankets, towels, or fabric, Asks to be hugged very tightly — 'harder!', Squeezes into small spaces (between furniture, in closets, under beds), Burrows under couch cushions or pillows, Sleeps better with heavy blanket or when tightly tucked.

mainEntity.name: How does a Compression Sheet / Sensory Bed Sheet help with deep pressure seeking?

acceptedAnswer.text: A compression sheet is a stretchy, fitted sheet that wraps tightly around the child and mattress, providing sustained deep pressure throughout sleep. Unlike weighted blankets (which provide downward pressure), compression sheets provide circumferential squeeze — pressure from all sides like a constant hug. For children who love wrapping themselves tightly, this provides the sensation they seek in a safe, breathable format that allows movement and won't bunch up or become a safety hazard. Price: ₹1,500–4,000. DIY option: Use a fitted sheet 1-2 sizes too small for the mattress (creates natural tension). Or sew a tube of stretchy lycra/spandex fabric sized to wrap snugly around child and mattress. Resistance band wrapped around mattress under regular sheet can provide compression effect. Safety: Ensure face is always free and unobstructed. Child should be able to exit sheet independently. Check that compression isn't too tight — snug, not restrictive. Monitor for overheating. Not recommended for children under 2 or those who cannot reposition themselves.

mainEntity.name: How does a Body Sock / Sensory Sock help with deep pressure seeking?

acceptedAnswer.text: A body sock is a stretchy fabric envelope the child climbs inside. As they move, stretch, and push against the fabric, they receive deep pressure input across their entire body. For children who love tight wrapping, the body sock provides the containment and pressure they crave while allowing movement and play. They can see through the fabric, move around, and control how much pressure they receive by pushing harder or softer against the stretchy material. Price: ₹800–2,000. DIY option: Sew a tube from stretchy lycra/spandex fabric with opening at one end for entry. Size should be snug when child is inside — the stretch creates the pressure. Large stretchy pillowcase can work for small children (cut head hole). Key is the RESISTANCE of the fabric. Safety: Always supervise body sock use. Ensure child can breathe freely — head should be visible or sock should have face opening. Don't force use if child feels trapped or panics. Check for fraying or tears. Child should be able to exit independently.

mainEntity.name: How does a Weighted Blanket help with deep pressure seeking?

acceptedAnswer.text: Weighted blankets provide sustained deep pressure through evenly distributed weight across the body. This activates the proprioceptive system and triggers a calming parasympathetic response. For children who wrap themselves tightly in regular blankets, weighted blankets deliver similar deep pressure input without the wrapping — the weight provides the 'squeeze' they're seeking. The pressure helps the nervous system feel grounded and secure. Price: ₹1,500–5,000. DIY option: Sew channels in a duvet cover or fabric pouch and fill with plastic pellets, glass beads, or rice (distributed evenly). Standard guideline: approximately 10% of child's body weight plus 1-2 pounds. Always consult OT for appropriate weight recommendation. Safety: Never use for children under 2 years. Weight should not exceed 10% of child's body weight. Child must be able to remove blanket independently. Never cover face. Monitor for overheating. Consult OT for appropriate weight.

mainEntity.name: How does a Lycra Resistance Tunnel / Squeeze Machine help with deep pressure seeking?

acceptedAnswer.text: A lycra tunnel (or 'squeeze machine' inspired by Temple Grandin's original design) allows the child to crawl through a tube of stretchy fabric that provides deep pressure from all sides. Unlike a body sock (which encloses them), the tunnel is an activity — they enter one end and experience compression as they push through. For children who love squeezing into tight spaces, this provides a safe, purposeful way to get that full-body squeeze. Price: ₹1,500–4,000. DIY option: Sew a tube from stretchy lycra/spandex fabric wide enough for child to crawl through but snug enough to provide compression. Attach ends to stable frames or hang between two points. Can use large resistance band tunnels. Pool noodle frame with stretchy fabric wrapped around creates similar effect. Safety: Ensure tunnel is stable and won't collapse. Child should be able to exit from either end. Supervise use. Check for adequate ventilation. Not for children who panic in enclosed spaces. Ensure fabric doesn't bunch around face.

mainEntity.name: How does a Compression Vest / Compression Clothing help with deep pressure seeking?

acceptedAnswer.text: Compression garments provide constant, gentle squeeze around the torso (or full body) that can be worn throughout the day. For children who seek tight wrapping, compression wear provides similar deep pressure input invisibly under regular clothes. This allows them to receive the calming input they need during school, outings, or activities where wrapping in blankets isn't possible. Price: ₹1,000–3,500. DIY option: Tight-fitting lycra/spandex undershirts or athletic compression wear provide similar input. Layer tight camisole or compression shorts under regular clothes. Must be snug but not restrictive. Ace bandage wrapped around torso (with supervision) can provide temporary compression. Safety: Garment should be snug but never restrictive of breathing or circulation. Child should be able to put on and remove independently. Monitor for overheating. Not worn 24/7 — take breaks. Check sizing regularly as child grows.

mainEntity.name: How does a Pea Pod / Sensory Peapod Chair help with deep pressure seeking?

acceptedAnswer.text: A peapod is an inflatable or foam-filled pod that the child climbs inside and zips up, creating a contained, pressure-providing space. It combines the tight-space seeking these children love with gentle compression from the pod walls. Unlike squeezing between furniture (hard, unsafe), the peapod is soft, safe, and designed for this purpose. Children can read, rest, or calm down inside their 'cocoon.' Price: ₹3,000–8,000. DIY option: Large bean bag with a slit opening that child can crawl inside. Inflatable chair modified with pillows for compression. Pile of large pillows that child can burrow into and be covered by. Hanging fabric hammock that wraps around child. The key is enclosed, soft, pressure-providing space. Safety: Ensure adequate ventilation — child should never have face fully enclosed. Child should be able to exit independently. Check for overheating. Supervise younger children. Ensure zipper or closure isn't a pinch hazard.

mainEntity.name: What are the warning signs that need professional help?

acceptedAnswer.text: REAL WORLD EVIDENCE from Pinnacle Blooms Network: 20M+ Exclusive 1:1 Sessions. 97%+ Measured Improvement. 70+ Centers.

@graph.name: How to Help a Child with Deep Pressure Seeking
@graph.description: A graded approach using evidence-based materials. Deep pressure seeking is self-regulation — the child's nervous system is actively trying to calm itself. The wrapping, squeezing, and pressure-seeking behaviors serve a function. Treatment involves PROVIDING appropriate deep pressure input rather than trying to stop the seeking behaviors. When needs are met through appropriate tools, the desperate seeking decreases and organized self-regulation emerges.

step.text: A sensory processing pattern where the child's nervous system requires deep, sustained pressure input to feel calm, organized, and secure. Deep pressure activates the parasympathetic nervous system, creating a calming effect. Children with this pattern actively seek tight wrapping, compression, squeezing into spaces, and firm hugging because this input helps their brain feel where their body is and activates their calming system. Treatment involves PROVIDING appropriate deep pressure input rather than trying to stop the seeking behaviors. When needs are met through appropriate tools, the desperate seeking decreases and organized self-regulation emerges.

item.name: Compression Sheet / Sensory Bed Sheet
item.description: A compression sheet is a stretchy, fitted sheet that wraps tightly around the child and mattress, providing sustained deep pressure throughout sleep. Unlike weighted blankets (which provide downward pressure), compression sheets provide circumferential squeeze — pressure from all sides like a constant hug. For children who love wrapping themselves tightly, this provides the sensation they seek in a safe, breathable format that allows movement and won't bunch up or become a safety hazard.

additionalProperty.name: DIY Option
additionalProperty.value: Use a fitted sheet 1-2 sizes too small for the mattress (creates natural tension). Or sew a tube of stretchy lycra/spandex fabric sized to wrap snugly around child and mattress. Resistance band wrapped around mattress under regular sheet can provide compression effect.

additionalProperty.name: Safety Note
additionalProperty.value: Ensure face is always free and unobstructed. Child should be able to exit sheet independently. Check that compression isn't too tight — snug, not restrictive. Monitor for overheating. Not recommended for children under 2 or those who cannot reposition themselves.

item.name: Body Sock / Sensory Sock
item.description: A body sock is a stretchy fabric envelope the child climbs inside. As they move, stretch, and push against the fabric, they receive deep pressure input across their entire body. For children who love tight wrapping, the body sock provides the containment and pressure they crave while allowing movement and play. They can see through the fabric, move around, and control how much pressure they receive by pushing harder or softer against the stretchy material.

additionalProperty.name: DIY Option
additionalProperty.value: Sew a tube from stretchy lycra/spandex fabric with opening at one end for entry. Size should be snug when child is inside — the stretch creates the pressure. Large stretchy pillowcase can work for small children (cut head hole). Key is the RESISTANCE of the fabric.

additionalProperty.name: Safety Note
additionalProperty.value: Always supervise body sock use. Ensure child can breathe freely — head should be visible or sock should have face opening. Don't force use if child feels trapped or panics. Check for fraying or tears. Child should be able to exit independently.

item.name: Weighted Blanket
item.description: Weighted blankets provide sustained deep pressure through evenly distributed weight across the body. This activates the proprioceptive system and triggers a calming parasympathetic response. For children who wrap themselves tightly in regular blankets, weighted blankets deliver similar deep pressure input without the wrapping — the weight provides the 'squeeze' they're seeking. The pressure helps the nervous system feel grounded and secure.

additionalProperty.name: DIY Option
additionalProperty.value: Sew channels in a duvet cover or fabric pouch and fill with plastic pellets, glass beads, or rice (distributed evenly). Standard guideline: approximately 10% of child's body weight plus 1-2 pounds. Always consult OT for appropriate weight recommendation.

additionalProperty.name: Safety Note
additionalProperty.value: Never use for children under 2 years. Weight should not exceed 10% of child's body weight. Child must be able to remove blanket independently. Never cover face. Monitor for overheating. Consult OT for appropriate weight.

item.name: Lycra Resistance Tunnel / Squeeze Machine
item.description: A lycra tunnel (or 'squeeze machine' inspired by Temple Grandin's original design) allows the child to crawl through a tube of stretchy fabric that provides deep pressure from all sides. Unlike a body sock (which encloses them), the tunnel is an activity — they enter one end and experience compression as they push through. For children who love squeezing into tight spaces, this provides a safe, purposeful way to get that full-body squeeze.

additionalProperty.name: DIY Option
additionalProperty.value: Sew a tube from stretchy lycra/spandex fabric wide enough for child to crawl through but snug enough to provide compression. Attach ends to stable frames or hang between two points. Can use large resistance band tunnels. Pool noodle frame with stretchy fabric wrapped around creates similar effect.

additionalProperty.name: Safety Note
additionalProperty.value: Ensure tunnel is stable and won't collapse. Child should be able to exit from either end. Supervise use. Check for adequate ventilation. Not for children who panic in enclosed spaces. Ensure fabric doesn't bunch around face.

item.name: Compression Vest / Compression Clothing
item.description: Compression garments provide constant, gentle squeeze around the torso (or full body) that can be worn throughout the day. For children who seek tight wrapping, compression wear provides similar deep pressure input invisibly under regular clothes. This allows them to receive the calming input they need during school, outings, or activities where wrapping in blankets isn't possible.

additionalProperty.name: DIY Option
additionalProperty.value: Tight-fitting lycra/spandex undershirts or athletic compression wear provide similar input. Layer tight camisole or compression shorts under regular clothes. Must be snug but not restrictive. Ace bandage wrapped around torso (with supervision) can provide temporary compression.

additionalProperty.name: Safety Note
additionalProperty.value: Garment should be snug but never restrictive of breathing or circulation. Child should be able to put on and remove independently. Monitor for overheating. Not worn 24/7 — take breaks. Check sizing regularly as child grows.

item.name: Pea Pod / Sensory Peapod Chair
item.description: A peapod is an inflatable or foam-filled pod that the child climbs inside and zips up, creating a contained, pressure-providing space. It combines the tight-space seeking these children love with gentle compression from the pod walls. Unlike squeezing between furniture (hard, unsafe), the peapod is soft, safe, and designed for this purpose. Children can read, rest, or calm down inside their 'cocoon.'

additionalProperty.name: DIY Option
additionalProperty.value: Large bean bag with a slit opening that child can crawl inside. Inflatable chair modified with pillows for compression. Pile of large pillows that child can burrow into and be covered by. Hanging fabric hammock that wraps around child. The key is enclosed, soft, pressure-providing space.

additionalProperty.name: Safety Note
additionalProperty.value: Ensure adequate ventilation — child should never have face fully enclosed. Child should be able to exit independently. Check for overheating. Supervise younger children. Ensure zipper or closure isn't a pinch hazard.

item.name: Burrito Blanket / Wrap Blanket
item.description: For children who naturally wrap themselves in blankets, a burrito blanket is designed specifically for this purpose. These oversized, soft blankets are made to be wrapped tightly around the child like a burrito — the activity they're already doing naturally. Some have velcro or straps to maintain the tight wrap. This validates their seeking behavior and provides a blanket optimized for wrapping rather than fighting with regular blankets.

additionalProperty.name: DIY Option
additionalProperty.value: Large, soft fleece blanket (oversized for child) works well. Microfiber or minky fabric holds wrap better than cotton. Can add velcro strips to maintain wrap. Weighted blanket material makes a weighted burrito wrap. Large muslin swaddle blankets also work for smaller children.

additionalProperty.name: Safety Note
additionalProperty.value: Never wrap so tight that breathing is restricted. Face must always be free. Child should be able to unwrap themselves. Don't leave wrapped child unattended. Watch for overheating.

item.name: Cuddle Swing / Therapy Swing with Cocoon
item.description: A cuddle swing (or cocoon swing) is a fabric swing that wraps around the child, providing deep pressure from the fabric while also offering vestibular input from swinging. For children who love tight wrapping, the swing provides containment and pressure while adding the calming effect of rhythmic movement. The combination of deep pressure + gentle swinging is highly regulating for many sensory seekers.

additionalProperty.name: DIY Option
additionalProperty.value: Hang a large, stretchy fabric from a ceiling point, creating a hammock/cocoon child can climb into. Large scarf or fabric wrap hung from a bar. Lycra fabric hammock. DIY platform swing with fabric wrapped around seated child. Key is secure hanging point rated for movement and weight.

additionalProperty.name: Safety Note
additionalProperty.value: Install only in appropriate ceiling mount rated for swinging weight. Supervise all swing use. Ensure child cannot fall out. Check fabric and mounting hardware regularly. Height should allow child to touch ground if needed.

item.name: Pressure Wrap / Ace Bandage System
item.description: For children who seek tight wrapping, adjustable pressure wraps allow customized compression on specific body parts or the whole body. Unlike fixed-compression garments, wraps can be adjusted for more or less pressure based on what the child needs in the moment. This gives the child (and parent) control over the intensity of input and teaches the child to recognize and request what their body needs.

additionalProperty.name: DIY Option
additionalProperty.value: Ace bandages or elastic wrap bandages work well — adjust tightness by wrapping more or less firmly. Resistance bands wrapped around body (supervised). Long scarves or fabric strips. Compression sleeves cut from old compression garments. The key is adjustability.

additionalProperty.name: Safety Note
additionalProperty.value: Never wrap tight enough to restrict circulation (check for color change, numbness, tingling). Don't wrap around neck or face. Ensure child can remove or loosen independently. Check frequently for proper circulation. Don't leave wrapped child unattended for extended periods.

socialMedia.caption: If your child wraps themselves so tight in blankets, squeezes into tiny spaces, and can't relax without pressure — their nervous system needs deep input to feel safe. It's not weird. It's regulation. Here are 9 materials that give them the squeeze their body is searching for. Save this for your pressure-seeking kiddo. 💜 #DeepPressure #SensorySeeking #PinnacleBlooms
